title: Type I vs Type II error
description: Distinguish between Type I and Type II error in context.
topic_path: Type I vs Type II error

assessment_items.question_content:
Regulations from the Environmental Protection Agency say that soil used in play areas should not have lead levels that exceed $400$ parts per million (ppm). Before beginning construction at a new site, an agent will take a sample of soil and run a significance test on the mean lead level in the soil. If the mean lead level in the sample is significantly higher than $400\text{ ppm}$, then the soil is deemed unsafe and construction cannot continue. Here are the hypotheses for this test:

$\begin{align}
H_0&: \mu \leq 400 \text{ ppm (soil is safe)} \\
H_{\text{a}}&: \mu > 400 \text{ ppm (soil is unsafe)}
\end{align}$

(where $\mu$ is the mean lead level in the soil at the new site).

**Which of the following would be a Type I error in this setting?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Type I and Type II error

 | $H_0 $ true | $H_\text{a}$ true
- | - | -
**Fail to reject** $H_0$ | correct conclusion | Type II error
**Reject** $H_0$ | Type I error | correct conclusion

###In this setting

**Type I error: Rejecting a true null hypothesis**

If $H_0$ is true, then the soil is actually safe. So a Type I error would occur if the sample result is significantly higher than $400\text{ ppm}$, and construction stops when the soil is actually safe.

**Type II error: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis**

If $H_0$ is false, then $H_{\text{a}}$ is true, and the soil is actually unsafe. So a Type II error would occur if the sample result isn't significantly higher than $400\text{ ppm}$ and construction continues when the soil is actually unsafe.

###Answer

In this setting, a Type I error would be:

**The soil is actually safe, and the sample result is significantly higher than $400\text{ ppm}$, so construction stops.**

choices.content: The soil is actually safe, and the sample result is below $400\text{ ppm}$, so construction continues.
The soil is actually safe, and the sample result is significantly higher than $400\text{ ppm}$, so construction stops.
The soil is actually unsafe, and the sample result is below $400\text{ ppm}$, so construction continues.
The soil is actually unsafe, and the sample result is significantly higher than $400\text{ ppm}$, so construction stops.

assessment_items.question_content:
A large nationwide poll recently showed an unemployment rate of $9\%$ in the US. The mayor of a local town wonders if this national result holds true for her town, so she plans on taking a sample of her residents to see if the unemployment rate is significantly different than $9\%$ in her town.

Let $p$ represent the unemployment rate in her town. Here are the hypotheses she'll use:

$\begin{align}
H_0&: p=0.09 \\
H_{\text{a}}&: p \neq 0.09
\end{align}$

**Under which of the following conditions would the mayor commit a Type I error?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Type I and Type II error

 | $H_0 $ true | $H_\text{a}$ true
- | - | -
**Fail to reject** $H_0$ | correct conclusion | Type II error
**Reject** $H_0$ | Type I error | correct conclusion

###In this setting

**Type I error: Rejecting a true null hypothesis**

If $H_0$ is true, then the town's rate is actually $9\%$. So a Type I error would occur if the sample result is significantly different than $9\%$, and she concludes that the town's rate is not $9\%$.

**Type II error: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis**

If $H_0$ is false, then $H_{\text{a}}$ is true, and the town's rate is actually not $9\%$. So a Type II error would occur if the sample result isn't significantly different than $9\%$, and she doesn't conclude that the town's rate is different than $9\%$.

###Answer

In this setting, a Type I error would be:

**She concludes the town's unemployment rate is not $9\%$ when it actually is.**

choices.content: She concludes the town's unemployment rate is not $9\%$ when it actually is.
She concludes the town's unemployment rate is not $9\%$ when it actually is not.
She concludes the town's unemployment rate is $9\%$ when it actually is.
She concludes the town's unemployment rate is $9\%$ when it actually is not.

assessment_items.question_content:
Donated blood is tested for infectious diseases and other contaminants. Since most donated blood is safe, it saves time and money to test batches of donated blood rather than test individual samples. A certain test is performed to see if a certain toxin is present, and the entire batch is discarded if the toxin is detected. This is similar to using a null and an alternative hypothesis to determine whether to discard or keep the batch. The hypotheses being tested could be stated as:

$H_0:$ The batch does not contain the toxin.  
$H_\text{a}:$ The batch contains the toxin.

**What would be the consequence of a Type I error in this context?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Type I and Type II error

 | $H_0 $ true | $H_\text{a}$ true
- | - | -
**Fail to reject** $H_0$ | correct conclusion | Type II error
**Reject** $H_0$ | Type I error | correct conclusion

###In this setting

**Type I error: Rejecting a true null hypothesis**

If $H_0$ is true, then the batch does not actually contain the toxin. So a Type I error would occur if the test detects the toxin and the batch is discarded.

**Type II error: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis**

If $H_0$ is false, then $H_\text{a}$ is true, and the batch does actually contain the toxin. So a Type II error would occur if the test doesn't detect the toxin and the batch is kept.

###Answer

The consequences of a Type I error would be:

**The batch is discarded when it actually doesn't contain the toxin.**

choices.content: The batch is discarded when it actually contains the toxin.
The batch is discarded when it actually doesn't contain the toxin.
The batch is kept when it actually contains the toxin.
The batch is kept when it actually doesn't contain the toxin.

assessment_items.question_content:
A large university is curious if they should build another cafeteria. They plan to survey a sample of their students to see if there is strong evidence that the proportion interested in a meal plan is higher than $40\%$, in which case they will consider building a new cafeteria.

Let $p$ represent the proportion of students interested in a meal plan. Here are the hypotheses they'll use:

$\begin{align}
H_0&: p \leq 0.40 \\
H_{\text{a}}&: p > 0.40
\end{align}$

**Which of the following would be a Type I error in this setting?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Type I and Type II error

 | $H_0 $ true | $H_\text{a}$ true
- | - | -
**Fail to reject** $H_0$ | correct conclusion | Type II error
**Reject** $H_0$ | Type I error | correct conclusion

###In this setting

**Type I error: Rejecting a true null hypothesis**

If $H_0$ is true, then at most $40\%$ of students are actually interested. So a Type I error would occur if the sample result is significantly higher than $40\%$, and they conclude that more than $40\%$ are interested.

**Type II error: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis**

If $H_0$ is false, then $H_{\text{a}}$ is true, and more than $40\%$ of students are actually interested. So a Type II error would occur if the sample result is not significantly higher than $40\%$, and they don't conclude that more than $40\%$ are interested.

###Answer

In this setting, a Type I error would be:

**At most $40\%$ are actually interested, but they conclude that more than $40\%$ are interested.**

choices.content: More than $40\%$ are actually interested, and they don't conclude that more than $40\%$ are interested.
More than $40\%$ are actually interested, and they conclude that more than $40\%$ are interested.
At most $40\%$ are actually interested, and they conclude that more than $40\%$ are interested.
At most $40\%$ are actually interested, and they conclude that less than $40\%$ are interested.

assessment_items.question_content:
A quality control expert wants to test the null hypothesis that a new solar panel is no more effective than the older model.

**What would be the consequence of a Type I error in this context?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Type I and Type II error

 | $H_0 $ true | $H_\text{a}$ true
- | - | -
**Fail to reject** $H_0$ | correct conclusion | Type II error
**Reject** $H_0$ | Type I error | correct conclusion

###In this setting

**Type I error: Rejecting a true null hypothesis**

If $H_0$ is true, then the new panel is not more effective. So a Type I error would occur if they conclude it is more effective.

**Type II error: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis**

If $H_0$ is false, then $H_\text{a}$ is true, and the new panel is more effective. So a Type II error would occur if they don't conclude it is more effective.

###Answer

The consequences of a Type I error would be:

**They conclude the new panel is more effective when it actually is not more effective.**

choices.content: They don't conclude the new panel is more effective when it actually is not more effective.
They don't conclude the new panel is more effective when it actually is more effective.
They conclude the new panel is more effective when it actually is more effective.
They conclude the new panel is more effective when it actually is not more effective.

assessment_items.question_content:
According to a report from the United States Environmental Protection Agency, burning one gallon of gasoline typically emits about $8.9\text{ kg}$ of $\text{CO}_2$. A fuel company wants to test a new type of gasoline designed to have lower $\text{CO}_2$ emissions. Here are their hypotheses:

$\begin{align}
H_0&: \mu =8.9 \text{ kg} \\
H_{\text{a}}&: \mu < 8.9 \text{ kg}
\end{align}$

(where $\mu$ is the mean amount of $\text{CO}_2$ emitted by burning one gallon of this new gasoline).

**Under which of the following conditions would the company commit a Type I error?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Type I and Type II error

 | $H_0 $ true | $H_\text{a}$ true
- | - | -
**Fail to reject** $H_0$ | correct conclusion | Type II error
**Reject** $H_0$ | Type I error | correct conclusion

###In this setting

**Type I error: Rejecting a true null hypothesis**

If $H_0$ is true, then the mean amount of $\text{CO}_2$ emitted is actually $8.9\text{ kg}$. So a Type I error would occur if the sample result is significantly lower than $8.9\text{ kg}$, and they conclude that emissions are lower than $8.9\text{ kg}$

**Type II error: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis**

If $H_0$ is false, then $H_\text{a}$ is true, and the mean amount of $\text{CO}_2$ emitted is actually lower than $8.9\text{ kg}$. So a Type II error would occur if the sample result is not significantly lower than $8.9\text{ kg}$, and they don't conclude that emissions are lower than $8.9\text{ kg}$

###Answer

In this setting, a Type I error would be:

**The mean amount of $\text{CO}_2$ emitted by the new fuel is actually $8.9\text{ kg}$, and they conclude it is lower than $8.9\text{ kg}$.**

choices.content: The mean amount of $\text{CO}_2$ emitted by the new fuel is actually $8.9\text{ kg}$, and they fail to conclude it is lower than $8.9\text{ kg}$.
The mean amount of $\text{CO}_2$ emitted by the new fuel is actually lower than $8.9\text{ kg}$, and they conclude it is lower than $8.9\text{ kg}$.
The mean amount of $\text{CO}_2$ emitted by the new fuel is actually $8.9\text{ kg}$, and they conclude it is lower than $8.9\text{ kg}$.
The mean amount of $\text{CO}_2$ emitted by the new fuel is actually lower than $8.9\text{ kg}$, and they fail to conclude it is lower than $8.9\text{ kg}$.

assessment_items.question_content:

Let $p$ represent the unemployment rate in her town. Here are the hypotheses she'll use:

$\begin{align}
H_0&: p=0.09 \\
H_{\text{a}}&: p \neq 0.09
\end{align}$

**Which of the following would be a Type II error in this setting?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Type I and Type II error

 | $H_0 $ true | $H_\text{a}$ true
- | - | -
**Fail to reject** $H_0$ | correct conclusion | Type II error

In this setting, a Type II error would be:

Let $p$ represent the proportion of students interested in a meal plan. Here are the hypotheses they'll use:

$\begin{align}
H_0&: p \leq 0.40 \\
H_{\text{a}}&: p > 0.40

- | - | -
**Fail to reject** $H_0$ | correct conclusion | Type II error

###In this setting

**Type I error: Rejecting a true null hypothesis**

If $H_0$ is true, then at most $40\%$ of students are actually interested. So a Type I error would occur if the sample result is significantly higher than $40\%$, and they consider building the new cafeteria when they shouldn't.

**Type II error: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis**

If $H_0$ is false, then $H_{\text{a}}$ is true, and more than $40\%$ of students are actually interested. So a Type II error would occur if the sample result is not significantly higher than $40\%$, and they don't consider building the new cafeteria when they should.

assessment_items.question_content:
A quality control expert wants to test the null hypothesis that a new solar panel is no more effective than the older model.

###Type I and Type II error

 | $H_0 $ true | $H_\text{a}$ true
- | - | -
**Fail to reject** $H_0$ | correct conclusion | Type II error

If $H_0$ is true, then the new panel is not more effective. So a Type I error would occur if they conclude it is more effective.

**Type II error: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis**

If $H_0$ is false, then $H_\text{a}$ is true, and the new panel is more effective. So a Type II error would occur if they don't conclude it is more effective.

###Answer

In this setting, a Type II error would be:

**The new panel is actually more effective, and they don't conclude that it is more effective.**
